questions.question: Why might the nearest available driver NOT be the optimal match for a rider request?
options: A globally optimal assignment considers all active riders and drivers simultaneously; the nearest driver for one rider might be the only nearby driver for three other riders, making a slightly farther assignment better for total system wait time.
The nearest driver always has lower ratings than drivers slightly further away.
GPS coordinates are imprecise, so 'nearest' is not deterministic.
Uber charges more for shorter pickup distances, so nearest-driver matching would reduce revenue.
questions.explanation: Greedy nearest-driver is locally optimal but globally suboptimal. Batch matching with the Hungarian algorithm minimizes total system wait across all simultaneous riders and drivers.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a 500ms batch matching window over immediate per-request matching?
options: Collecting requests into a batch allows solving a bipartite assignment problem that minimizes total wait time across all riders and drivers simultaneously, which individual greedy matching cannot achieve.
The 500ms window allows the ETA model to warm up its cache before computing distances.
Batching reduces the number of Kafka messages by grouping multiple trip requests into a single event.
The 500ms window gives drivers time to manually accept or decline before the algorithm assigns them.
questions.explanation: Batch matching reframes individual assignments into a global optimization problem. The 500ms window is invisible to riders but enables a fundamentally better algorithm.

questions.question: DISCO sends a match offer to the top-3 drivers simultaneously rather than offering to each driver sequentially. Why?
options: Sequential offering adds 15s × N latency for each declined offer; sending to top-3 simultaneously caps worst-case acceptance latency at one 15s window regardless of how many of the three decline.
Simultaneous offers are required by local transportation regulations in most cities.
The matching algorithm cannot rank drivers by ETA accurately enough to determine a single best driver.
Simultaneous offers prevent drivers from gaming the acceptance timer by waiting until the last second.
questions.explanation: Sequential waterfall adds N×15s of potential delay. Hedging with top-3 simultaneously means even if two decline, the rider's wait grows by at most one 15s window.

questions.question: A dispatch matching worker crashes while processing a 500ms batch. How does Uber's system recover?
options: The Kafka consumer offset was not committed for that batch, so a new worker re-consumes the same events and reprocesses the batch from scratch.
The Cadence workflow saves the batch state every 100ms, so the new worker resumes from the last checkpoint.
The Redis cell store caches the match results, and the new worker reads from there on restart.
The riders in the lost batch automatically retry their requests after a 30-second client timeout.
questions.explanation: Kafka's at-least-once delivery with uncommitted offsets guarantees the batch is re-consumed on worker restart. This is why matching workers must be idempotent — the same trip request may be processed twice.

questions.question: Uber's ETA model reports 35% average error over the past 5 minutes. What should the dispatch system do?
options: Fall back to map-only routing for ETA computation and alert on-call; the ML model's predictions are less accurate than the base routing engine at this error level.
Increase the batch matching window to 2 seconds to compensate for the degraded ETA quality.
Switch to greedy nearest-driver matching to avoid relying on ETA estimates entirely.
Halt all new trip matching until the ML model is retrained and redeployed.
questions.explanation: Map-only ETA is the fallback when the ML layer degrades. Halting dispatch is unacceptable; the system must degrade gracefully.

questions.question: Why does Uber run separate matching pools for UberX, UberBlack, and UberXL instead of one unified pool?
options: Each pool enforces hard vehicle-type filters, separate SLA targets, and independent scaling; merging them would require complex compatibility checks on every candidate and risk cross-pool contamination of SLA metrics.
Separate pools are required because the Hungarian algorithm cannot handle heterogeneous vehicle types in the cost matrix.
Separate pools reduce Kafka throughput by allowing each pool to use a dedicated topic with fewer partitions.
UberBlack drivers are contractually prohibited from appearing in the same dispatch queue as UberX drivers.
questions.explanation: Pool isolation enforces type contracts, allows pool-specific tuning (e.g., UberBlack has a longer acceptance window), and prevents a surge in UberX demand from degrading UberBlack SLAs.

questions.question: What is the role of supply forecasting in DISCO's pre-positioning strategy?
options: By predicting driver deficits in specific H3 cells 5-15 minutes ahead (e.g., a concert ending), DISCO increases surge pricing early to attract drivers before demand spikes — smoothing the supply-demand curve rather than reacting after the fact.
Supply forecasting determines the batch window size: more supply = shorter window, less supply = longer window.
Supply forecasting pre-caches ETA computations for predicted pickup locations to reduce match latency.
Supply forecasting identifies drivers who are likely to go offline soon and deprioritizes them in match scoring.
questions.explanation: Pre-positioning incentives based on forecasts smooth supply-demand curves. Reacting after a concert ends means riders wait; attracting drivers 10 minutes early means coverage is ready when demand hits.

questions.question: A driver accepts a trip offer and immediately goes offline (GPS lost for 30 seconds). What does the dispatch system do?
options: After the 30-second heartbeat timeout, the driver is removed from the active pool and the trip is re-dispatched to the next-best candidate; the driver may receive a penalty for the cancellation.
The trip remains matched to the offline driver indefinitely until they reconnect.
The rider is immediately notified to request a new trip because the match is invalidated.
The Cadence workflow retries the driver offer up to 5 times over 60 seconds before re-dispatching.
questions.explanation: A 30-second heartbeat SLA prevents riders from being stranded waiting for a driver who may never reconnect. Re-dispatch keeps the system moving; penalties discourage frivolous offline events.
